topic: Someone discovers their neighbor is secretly wealthy
raw_output:
[story 1]

Eleanor had always considered her neighbor, Mr. Henderson, to be painfully mundane. His lawn was impeccably manicured, his hedges sculpted with an almost aggressive precision, and his car, a sleek obsidian thing, gleamed under the afternoon sun. It was just… excessive. One Tuesday, while retrieving her recycling bin, Eleanor noticed a delivery van pulling up to Mr. Henderson’s driveway. A silver sedan, far too expensive for the quiet cul-de-sac, pulled out, and a man in a crisp suit emerged, shaking hands with Mr. Henderson. The sheer spectacle made Eleanor’s jaw tighten. She watched the exchange, her knuckles turning white where she gripped the bin handle. Later that evening, as she watered her wilting petunias, she found herself staring at the pristine gate. A cold knot formed in her stomach, a sharp feeling of being utterly misplaced in this neighborhood.

[story 2]

"Honestly, the audacity," muttered Sarah to her friend over coffee. "Mr. Davies next door. I keep expecting him to drop a yacht on the porch." Sarah tapped her mug repeatedly, the ceramic rattling slightly. She had always viewed Mr. Davies as a quiet, slightly fussy man who tended his prize-winning roses. But yesterday, she saw it—a massive, unmarked truck idling near his property, and a woman in designer sunglasses stepping out, looking thoroughly unconcerned. Sarah felt a flush creep up her neck. She felt a sudden, intense need to check her own bank statement, a frantic impulse bubbling up. When Mr. Davies waved politely, his smile seeming impossibly wide, Sarah managed a tight, brittle nod, careful not to let her gaze linger on the expensive leather briefcase he carried.

[story 3]

I walked past the Henderson property twice that week, trying to maintain a façade of utter indifference. It wasn't indifference; it was a simmering resentment that made my shoulders tense beneath my sweater. Every time I saw the gleaming architecture behind the wrought-iron fence, a tightness seized my chest. I remember standing on my porch, watching a gardener—clearly not a casual hobbyist—direct workers around a newly installed fountain. The sound of the heavy machinery seemed to mock the quiet rhythm of my own life. I turned abruptly, walking back toward my house, my steps quick and uneven. I wanted to confront him, to ask what kind of secret extravagance he was hiding, but the words caught in my throat, replaced by a burning sensation behind my eyes.
